storm.summary_layperson: No storm is on radar right now, but the hot muggy air has loaded enough energy for afternoon thunderstorms to develop — the cool Long Island Sound tends to weaken storms before they reach the cove, so neighbors a mile inland may see more than you will.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water at 70°F against 99°F air creates a 28°F gap that builds a strong marine stable layer over the water, typically choking storm intensity before it reaches the shoreline.
storm.detail_html:
No storm cells are currently detectable on KOKX radar — the approach sector is clear and no cell is closing on the cove. The risk is potential, not imminent: CAPE is 2120 J/kg with 0–6 km wind shear at 30 kt WNW, crossing the moderate-risk threshold even without active radar returns. The NWS Area Forecast Discussion confirms isolated-to-scattered thunderstorms are possible through Sunday, though forecaster confidence is explicitly low due to a complex convective-debris environment.

The cove has a meaningful local modifier working in its favor. Sound water sits at 70°F while air temperatures reach 99°F — a 28°F gap that builds a strong marine stable layer over Long Island Sound. Any convection crossing the Sound from the southwest, the most common approach track, must fight through this stabilizing layer. CAPE remains below the 3000 J/kg punch-through threshold, so the marine layer retains real choking power. If a storm develops or approaches, expect the cove to see gusty wind without the rain and thunder that inland areas a mile or two away will feel more acutely.

Storm approach geometry is unknown today — no cell with a measured bearing exists yet. A storm arriving from SW–NW (crossing the Sound perpendicularly) experiences the strongest protection benefit. One arriving from the north over land bypasses the Sound entirely; in that scenario NWS county-wide warnings apply directly to the cove with no marine-layer discount.

Shear of 30 kt sits in a gray zone — below the 35 kt threshold for punch-through intensification at the sea-breeze convergence boundary, but elevated enough that a well-organized cell could maintain structure crossing the Sound. The RAP model's shear estimate of 20 kt is more conservative; the spread between models adds uncertainty. Watch radar through the evening — if a 45+ dBZ cell appears within 30 miles on a SW bearing, reassess quickly.

Bottom line: moderate risk on paper due to CAPE and shear, but no current radar threat and a strong protective marine layer in place. Surrounding inland towns carry higher effective storm risk than the shoreline through sunset.

wind.tactics_blurb: SSW flow channels slightly more pressure along the west shore (Field Point / Belle Haven side) than near Tod's Point — work right early while the breeze is still ramping. Once it matures around 7 PM, play the ±10° oscillations around the SSW mean.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime A+ — thermal sea breeze reinforced by a synoptic SW/W tailwind. The inland–Sound temperature gap is 28.3°F (KHPN 99°F vs Sound 70°F), far above the 5°F threshold. The coastal station ensemble shows 91% southerly coverage with 0% inland, confirming a fully established sea breeze. The NWS forecast calls for “southwest wind around 8 mph,” consistent with a background onshore gradient that adds to rather than opposes the thermal. Regime A+ signature: stronger and longer-lasting than a pure thermal day.

Direction at RYC is currently SSW 208°, consistent with a mature western-LIS sea breeze. HRRR ran significantly behind this afternoon — the model showed SE 141° at the 4 PM hour while stations were already at SSW 210°, a 69° divergence. The maturation correction has been applied: near-term forecast direction corrects to S ~175° at 5 PM, then veers to SSW 205–210° by 6–7 PM as HRRR catches up to reality. Observed direction is trusted over raw model for current conditions; direction confidence is high.

The breeze is still in its ramping phase at 5 PM. During the first 2–3 hours of sea-breeze development, direction drifts persistently clockwise rather than oscillating cleanly. Expect a steady rightward trend from S → SSW through about 7 PM before the mature SB begins its regular ±10° oscillations around the SSW mean. Peak speed around 10 kt at 7 PM, easing to 5 kt by 9 PM.

Cove geometry with SSW flow produces relatively uniform conditions. The west shore (Field Point / Belle Haven) gets slightly more channeled pressure than the east shore near Tod's Point, which sits partially in the shadow of the Greenwich Point peninsula. No major holes expected — SSW is one of the more consistent directions for this cove. Start mid-line, work right to stay on the lifted tack during the persistent rightward-ramping phase, then shift to playing oscillations for the second race.

Sunset at approximately 8:19 PM ends pure thermal forcing within 30–60 minutes. Regime A+ extends this: the synoptic SW gradient of 8–10 kt survives after the thermal shuts down. Expect wind to hold at 5+ kt through about 9:00–9:30 PM, then drop below 3 kt. HRRR shows 5 kt at 9 PM and a mean of 3.4 kt in the extended outlook through 10 PM, consistent with a moderate gradient hold into the late evening.

data_caveats: APRS citizen-station network offline — SSL certificate error on aprs.fi; spatial wind ensemble uses METAR/WeatherLink/buoy sources only.
Buoy 44040 met obs stale (75.7 min old); SST reading from this afternoon remains valid as a marine-layer proxy.
